content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY                GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105
(415) 543-8555


February 7, 1990

Katherine Donovan, Esq.
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804

     Re: Request for Advice Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114

Dear Ms. Donovan:

     For the past several years, the Coastal Commission has sustained significant
reductions in its annual budget from the state. Those reductions have
directly and negatively affected the Commission's discharge of its
responsibilities. For example, because of travel costs, staff attendance at
off-site meetings has been curtailed. Another significant result of the
budget reductions has been a virtual cessation of all training opportunities
for Commission staff. Generally, training sessions involve fees, and may
also necessitate travel. The Commission simply does not have enough funding
to cover these expenses.

     From time to time, however, other government agencies have expressed a
willingness to facilitate Commission staff participation in certain
activities. In addition, certain training opportunities sponsored by other
agencies have arisen. To assure that the Commission and its officers and
employees fully comply with the provisions of the Political Reform Act, the
Coastal Commission hereby requests that the Fair Political Practices
Commission (FPPC) issue written advice pursuant to Government Code section
83114(b) with respect to these activities and opportunities.

     Attachment I, enclosed, describes several situations which have occurred and
others which we expect to encounter in the future. In addition, that
attachment identifies several specific questions regarding these situations,
as well as several questions regarding the FPPC's opinion in Stone, (1977) 3
FPPC Ops. 52. We have also enclosed a general description of the
Commission's activities and staff. (Attachment II.) We request that the
FPPC include in its opinion the discussion of the questions set forth in the
attachment, as well as any other relevant issues, and that the advice
identify any effects on the Commission's officers and employees, as well as
any effects on the Commission or its decisions. Because of the upcoming
deadline for the filing of statements of economic interest, we request that
the FPPC give priority in its response to the activities described in the

Katherine Donovan, Esq.
February 7, 1990
Page 2


background statement which occurred during the reporting period.

We appreciate the FPPC's assistance.  Should you have any questions, please
contact Carolyn Small, staff counsel, at (415) 543-8555.

                                    Sincerely,

                                    [signature]
                                    Peter M. Douglas
                                    Executive Director


Enclosures

ATTACHMENT I


BACKGROUND STATEMENT

     Because of the Coastal Commission's limited budget, staff activities
involving travel, as well as funding for training sessions and other
activities involving fees has been curtailed. As a result, staff has been
unable to participate in offsite informational meetings, training
conferences, meetings regarding specific projects, and various other
activities important to the performance of its work.


     Some of these events also involve other government agencies concerned
with similar issues, or having concurrent jurisdiction. These agencies have
at times offered travel funding, or payment or waiver of fees or expenses
for Commission staff, and the Commission anticipates similar offers in the
future. In addition, certain training opportunities sponsored by other
agencies have arisen.

     One potential source of outside funds or other assistance is other state
agencies. In the case of expenses and per diem provided by another state
agency, a standard state form would be used by the employee, and would be
submitted to the state agency providing the funding.

     Specific examples are described below.

Working meetings--staff attendance at working meetings involving
particular projects or issues. These meetings are often held in cities
other than the employee's headquarters, necessitating that travel and
per diem be provided.

     Conferences, training sessions, workshops, field trips--training or
informational sessions relating directly to Commission
responsibilities. Employee travel and per diem may be provided, and
fees may be paid or waived by other agencies. In some instances, an
outside agency may sponsor a conference or other proceeding, paying for
facilities, materials, and/or consultants.

     One agency which may provide opportunities for staff attendance at such
events is the Minerals Management Service (MMS), the agency within the
Department of the Interior with primary federal responsibility for oil
and gas projects on the outer continental shelf. Among MMS'
responsibilities is the review and approval of outer continental shelf
oil and gas plans. The Commission receives consistency certifications
for these plans. Commission staff review and analyze the plans, and
prepare staff recommendations for the Commission's consideration. Staff
also prepare proposed regulations, and various informational documents
dealing with oil and gas activities. The Commission also conducts
consistency review of proposals to be carried out or funded by various
agencies within the Department of the Interior.

     One such potential conference is a seminar on conflict resolution likely
to be sponsored by MMS later this year. The conference would address
techniques for the evaluation and resolution of resource issues, and
seek to enhance working relationships among the agencies which review

                                    I - 1

various aspects of oil and gas projects, and with industry, by providing
a forum for discussion of issues, and through the training provided.
For the potential upcoming conference, MMS would like management
officials of the Commission, as well as several regular staff members,
to attend. As noted, MMS will be sponsoring the conference, e.g.,
paying for conference facilities and consultants, with other
participants paying only travel and daily expenses. For those attending
from the Commission, MMS would possibly pay for travel and per diem as
well. The Commission requests that the FPPC's response include a
discussion of any considerations raised by the potential participation
of Commission officials and employees in this conference.

A similar conference was sponsored by MMS last year. A Commission
staff attorney Dorothy Dickey, who attended, requests by this letter
that the FPPC provide specific advice with respect to any obligations
she may have under the Political Reform Act as a result of her
participation. Ms. Dickey has authorized the inclusion of this
request. The Commission paid for travel and lodging costs. At the
time, the employee was informed that there was no charge for the
conference, because MMS was sponsoring the event. Representatives of
other agencies and of industry attended, all without paying any
conference fees. The conference included background sessions on
conflict resolution, and discussion of how to apply the principles
presented to conflicts between the various agencies and industry
participant. An additional fact in this circumstance is that the
Commission employee's attendance resulted from a space becoming
available at the last minute, and thus arguably involved no cost to
MMS.

Site visits--visits by Commission staff to evaluate proposed project
sites. The agency providing the funds may be the project applicant or
may have concerns about, or an interest in, the project. Also, the
agency may have concurrent authority to review a project.

For example, another state agency would provide a sum for the travel
expenses of a Commission employee to visit a site where that agency
seeks to undertake a project. The funding would be provided directly to
the employee.

General review, reporting, and enforcement activities--Commission staff
review of large development projects or local coastal programs,
monitoring and enforcement, or review or preparation of reports
regarding specific issues, such as geologic stability, oil spills, or
fisheries impacts. Funding or other support may be provided for staff
or staff activities. Travel and per diem may be provided.

SPECIFIC QUESTIONS

1. Would the various scenarios described in the background statement be
fully covered by the provisions of Government Code section 82030(b)(2)? In
the FPPC's view, would "consideration of equal or greater value" for
purposes of Government Code section 82028 be provided by the staff

I - 2

attendance or participation described in that statement? Has there been any
change in the interpretation set forth in Advice Letter to Riddle, A-89-200,
with respect to the applicability of the exemption in section 82030(b)(2) to
gifts?

2. Does the exception for travel reimbursement in section 18728 of the
FPPC's regulations apply in the case of conferences, workshops, etc?

3. Would any of the scenarios described in the background statement result
in reporting requirements or constitute a potential basis for
disqualification under any categories not discussed in the responses above?
If so, we request that the FPPC identify the relevant categories.

4. If any of the situations described in the background statement would
result in reporting requirements or constitute a potential basis for
disqualification, what limitations would then apply to the scope of work of
the Commission employee involved? Specifically, in what circumstances is
there a material financial effect to a government entity? This address
affected where the other government agency is involved in a "proprietary"
capacity, e.g., where the government entity is the project applicant, or
submits a plan for required Commission review, or where actions of the
Commission could affect government revenues, such as oil and gas royalties
or property taxes accruing from the project to be reviewed?

5. With respect to the conflict resolution seminars described above, if
staff participation would result in reporting requirements or constitute a
potential basis for disqualification: (a) If the Commission selected the
employees who attended the conference, would that attendance fall
within the exception identified in the Stone opinion, (1977) 3 FPPC Ops.
522 (b) How would the value be calculated for reporting or disqualification
purposes for such activities in that no specific conference fees are
involved? (c) Is there any basis for a distinction for the employee
described above, who attended when a space became available at the last
minute?

6. In some circumstances, government agencies receive funds from project
applicants. If the Commission becomes aware that funds provided to the
Commission or used in the sponsorship of an event attended by staff were
derived from funds provided from a private person or entity, what, if any,
different considerations apply? If different considerations apply, in what
circumstances are funds attributable to their original source?

QUESTIONS REGARDING THE STONE OPINION

1. Are there any circumstances in which funding or other assistance
provided in a manner consistent with the Stone opinion, (1977) 3 FPPC Ops.
52, would be attributed to the Commissioners or to the executive staff
member who selects the participant?

2. In those circumstances where the activities described in the background
statement would result in a reportable interest or basis for
disqualification, would this result be affected by handling of the situation

I - 3

in the manner set forth in the Stone opinion? Where the funding or
invitation to participate is for a specifically identified purpose, and is
likely to be used by a particular, known employee because of the subject
matter or nature of the activity, is there any effect on the exception
allowed under Stone?

3. May the resolution called for in the Stone opinion be a resolution by
the Commission's executive director or by another member of the Commission's
management staff?




I - 4

ATTACHMENT II

GENERAL BACKGROUND

The California Coastal Commission carries out various responsibilities
relating to the management and development of coastal zone resources.
Within specified areas, the Commission reviews permit applications for
development proposals. The Commission also reviews local coastal programs
submitted by local governments, long range development plans submitted by
universities, and port master plans. As a matter, after
certification of a program for a particular area, some of the Commission's
permit review authority is delegated to the local government. The
Commission retains original permit jurisdiction with respect to development
in certain areas, e.g. state waters. In addition, in specified areas, and
for various types of projects, certain local permitting decisions are
subject to appellate review by the Commission. The Commission also conducts
enforcement activities relating to these functions. Commission staff
activities include the review and analysis of each of the types of proposals
described above, and the preparation of recommendations and proposed
findings for the Commission's consideration.

The Commission is the state's designated agency for purposes of the federal
Coastal Management Act (16 U.S.C. section 1451 et seq.) Pursuant to
this designation, the Commission reviews consistency determinations for
projects to be carried out by the federal government, and consistency
certifications for activities subject to federal permitting requirements,
including plans for oil and gas exploration on the outer continental shelf.
Again, staff's role is to review all proposals, and to prepare
recommendations and proposed findings for the Commission.

The Commission and its staff are also normally involved in various related
activities. For example, Commission staff prepare various informational
documents, such as a summary of oil and gas activities affecting the coastal
zone, and a newsletter for local governments. Commission staff participate
in review panels created to prepare environmental impact reports or
statements, and on committees researching or addressing various coastal
issues. Commission staff may also attend various training sessions related
to their work. Both Commissioners and Commission staff provide
informational services, including written and oral presentations.

The Commission's staff is largely comprised of coastal analysts. Several
specialists are included. For example, the Commission's staff includes an
engineer, a marine biologist, and a geologist. In general, the Commission
staff includes only one specialist in each subject matter area. The
Commission's staff also includes executive staff, attorneys, administrative
personnel, support staff, and interns.

The California Coastal Act is set forth in the California Public Resources
Code, beginning at section 30000. The Commission's regulations are set
forth in Title 14 of the California Code of Regulations, beginning at
section 13001.

embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION 631 HOWARD STREET, 4TH FLOOR SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105 (415) 543-8555 February 7, 1990 Katherine Donovan, Esq. Chief Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, California 95804 Re: Request for Advice Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114 Dear Ms. Donovan: For the past several years, the Coastal Commission has sustained significant reductions in its annual budget from the state. Those reductions have directly and negatively affected the Commission's discharge of its responsibilities. For example, because of travel costs, staff attendance at off-site meetings has been curtailed. Another significant result of the budget reductions has been a virtual cessation of all training opportunities for Commission staff. Generally, training sessions involve fees, and may also necessitate travel. The Commission simply does not have enough funding to cover these expenses. From time to time, however, other government agencies have expressed a willingness to facilitate Commission staff participation in certain activities. In addition, certain training opportunities sponsored by other agencies have arisen. To assure that the Commission and its officers and employees fully comply with the provisions of the Political Reform Act, the Coastal Commission hereby requests that the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) issue written advice pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b) with respect to these activities and opportunities. Attachment I, enclosed, describes several situations which have occurred and others which we expect to encounter in the future. In addition, that attachment identifies several specific questions regarding these situations, as well as several questions regarding the FPPC's opinion in Stone, (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 52. We have also enclosed a general description of the Commission's activities and staff. (Attachment II.) We request that the FPPC include in its opinion the discussion of the questions set forth in the attachment, as well as any other relevant issues, and that the advice identify any effects on the Commission's officers and employees, as well as any effects on the Commission or its decisions. Because of the upcoming deadline for the filing of statements of economic interest, we request that the FPPC give priority in its response to the activities described in the Katherine Donovan, Esq. February 7, 1990 Page 2 background statement which occurred during the reporting period. We appreciate the FPPC's assistance. Should you have any questions, please contact Carolyn Small, staff counsel, at (415) 543-8555. Sincerely, [signature] Peter M. Douglas Executive Director Enclosures ATTACHMENT I BACKGROUND STATEMENT Because of the Coastal Commission's limited budget, staff activities involving travel, as well as funding for training sessions and other activities involving fees has been curtailed. As a result, staff has been unable to participate in offsite informational meetings, training conferences, meetings regarding specific projects, and various other activities important to the performance of its work. Some of these events also involve other government agencies concerned with similar issues, or having concurrent jurisdiction. These agencies have at times offered travel funding, or payment or waiver of fees or expenses for Commission staff,
